article_title: FTC Fines Cox Media Group $880,000 for Selling Fake AI Eavesdropping Ad Service That Was Just Resold Email Lists
summary: Submission approvable with 1 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

content_preview.title: FTC Fines Cox Media Group $880,000 for Selling Fake AI Eavesdropping Ad Service That Was Just Resold Email Lists
content_preview.summary: The FTC settled for $930,000 total after CMG and two partners falsely claimed their 'Active Listening' service captured real-time smart-device voice data to deliver hyper-local ads — it was actually resold email lists.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The Federal Trade Commission announced on May 21, 2026 that CMG Media Corporation — which does business as Cox Media Group — and two smaller marketing partners must pay a combined $930,000 to settle charges that they deceived small-business customers by selling an advertising service that never worked as advertised. The service, branded "Active Listening," was marketed as an AI-powered tool that could capture real-time voice data from consumers' smart devices and deliver geographica...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News article at 936 words (within the 400-1200 word range for News). Strong narrative arc from the 2023 exposé through the FTC settlement. Clear, factual prose with appropriate section breaks. The Analysis section adds editorial value by contextualizing the case within a broader FTC enforcement trend.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 5 sources verified from disk snapshots. source-0.html.gz (Gizmodo FTC fines): confirmed settlement amounts ($880k CMG, $25k each partner), Mufarrige quotes, email list reselling finding, and consumer protection bar language. source-1.html.gz (Gizmodo pitch deck 2024): confirmed pitch deck mechanics quote, 470+ sources claim, Google/Amazon/Facebook partnership claims, and partner responses. source-2.html.gz (Gizmodo 2023 original): confirmed 'With Active Listening, CMG can now use voice data to target your advertising to the EXACT people you are looking for' marketing copy and 'AC is on it's last leg!' example. source-3.html.gz (The Register): confirmed Mufarrige quotes, $930k total, email broker reselling characterization, and permanent bans. source-4.html.gz (FTC press release): confirmed all settlement figures, FTC Act Section 5 analysis, consent mechanism rejection, and permanent prohibition terms. KEY FINDING: In The Settlement section, the article states 'A Cox Media Group spokesperson told [Gizmodo]' the quote beginning 'We are pleased to have this matter resolved...' — but source-0 (Gizmodo) explicitly attributes this quote to Wired, not Gizmodo. The quote is also truncated; Gizmodo's source adds 'We withdrew the materials expeditiously and stopped further use of the product.' This is a misattribution of the outlet and a partial quote — the substance of the spokesperson statement is real and verifiable. The automated 'AI self-reference' finding (pattern: as an ai) is a false positive — the match occurs in 'marketed as an AI-powered tool' which is a product description, not editorial self-reference.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All core facts verified against sources: the $930,000 total settlement, $880,000 CMG share, $25,000 each for MindSift and 1010 Digital Works, the email-list-reselling scheme, the FTC's May 21 2026 announcement date, the rejection of clickwrap consent as opt-in for voice surveillance, and the permanent prohibition terms. The Mufarrige quotes are verbatim from both FTC source and The Register. The pitch deck quotes are verbatim from Gizmodo source-1. The 2023 CMG marketing copy is verbatim from Gizmodo source-2. One misattribution found: the Cox spokesperson quote is attributed to Gizmodo but Gizmodo's own article says the spokesperson told Wired.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism in the body despite a sensational topic. The article does not adopt the FTC's characterizations without attribution — it consistently attributes findings to 'the FTC' or cites the press release. The Analysis section editorializes mildly but stays within appropriate bounds for opinion-flagged analysis.
editor_notes.originality: No duplicate found. A search of src/content/articles/2026-05/ confirms no prior coverage of Cox Media Group, the Active Listening service, or this FTC settlement. The article is original.
concerns: The Cox Media Group spokesperson quote in The Settlement section is misattributed: the article links to Gizmodo as the source, but Gizmodo's own article attributes the quote to Wired. The quote is also truncated — the Gizmodo snapshot shows the full statement includes 'We withdrew the materials expeditiously and stopped further use of the product.' which is omitted. A corrections record is warranted.
recommendations: File a corrections record noting that the Cox spokesperson quote was attributed to Gizmodo but should be attributed to Wired, and that the quote was truncated.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Substantively strong article on a newsworthy FTC enforcement action. The reporting is well-sourced and the main facts are accurate. One recoverable misattribution: the spokesperson quote is credited to Gizmodo when Gizmodo's own reporting credits Wired. APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS with a corrections record documenting the misattribution.
